canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, exploration efficiency is a construct characterizing the quality of an agent's search process during learning or tuning. One definition frames it as the degree to which a model generates "sufficiently diverse trajectories to support continued learning and improvement." This view is associated with the "exploration-exploitation tradeoff" and the prevention of what one paper calls "diversity collapse." A second, more specific definition describes it as the extent to which an agent's exploration focuses on "meaningful, action-altering variations rather than redundant or ineffective changes." This latter framing emphasizes a "targeted and efficient" process that prioritizes changes with a "higher likelihood of meaningful impact on action." Both perspectives highlight the need to find a "balance between per-iteration improvement and exploration efficiency" to optimize overall performance.
expert_removed_reason: method/engineering — not a construct/measurement/behavior of LLM ability
papers: Building Math Agents with Multi-Turn Iterative Preference Learning
CARTS: Advancing Neural Theorem Proving with Diversified Tactic Calibration and Bias-Resistant Tree Search
EVOLvE: Evaluating and Optimizing LLMs For In-Context Exploration
Towards Efficient Online Tuning of VLM Agents via Counterfactual Soft Reinforcement Learning

definitions.text: The degree to which a model or training process can generate sufficiently diverse trajectories to support continued learning and improvement.
definitions.paper: Building Math Agents with Multi-Turn Iterative Preference Learning

One definition frames it as the degree to which a model generates "sufficiently diverse trajectories to support continued learning and improvement." This view is associated with the "exploration-exploitation tradeoff" and the prevention of what one paper calls "diversity collapse." A second, more specific definition describes it as the extent to which an agent's exploration focuses on "meaningful, action-altering variations rather than redundant or ineffective changes." This latter framing emphasizes a "targeted and efficient" process that prioritizes changes with a "higher likelihood of meaningful impact on action."
